value.cross_market_context: normal regular session for both European and US venues; European equities followed a global tech-led risk-off move after weakness on Wall Street and rising US Federal Reserve rate-hike bets

text: European equity markets traded a normal regular session on Tuesday, June 23, 2026, and closed lower in a broad, tech-led risk-off move. Live closing prints are evidenced by the LSEG FTSE 100 daily table (close as of 16:35 BST on volume of about 886 million shares) and dated June 23 closes across the Trading Economics Euro Area, Germany and France pages. Daily moves are measured against the Monday, June 22 close. Unlike the prior report date, there is no US-holiday asymmetry: both European and US venues traded a normal session, and European equities followed weakness on Wall Street and rising US Federal Reserve rate-hike expectations.

stoxx_europe_600.basis: Trading Economics dated EU600 table 634.64 / -0.72%; Reuters wrap -0.7%; MarketScreener widget 634.63 / -0.73% (prior 639.27); corroborated across three vendors

euro_stoxx_50.basis: Trading Economics EU50 table 6,232.46 / -1.25%; MarketScreener -1.28%; CFD-tracked

dax.basis: Trading Economics CFD-tracked DE40 24,893.58 / -0.98% (previous 25,139.69); Reuters European cash-close wrap put the DAX at -0.3%; disclosed basis discrepancy

cac_40.basis: Trading Economics FR40 8,340.71 / -0.71% (close about 8,341); Reuters -0.7%; third straight down session

ftse_100.basis: Investors Chronicle/LSEG precise historical close; Reuters -0.2%

market_session: European regular close on 2026-06-23; FTSE 100 at the 16:35 BST London close

calculation.method: FTSE 100: June 23 close 10,428.85 minus June 22 close 10,437.85 equals -9.00 points (-0.09%); continental percentages are Trading Economics CFD-tracked day-changes vs the prior session, corroborated by the Reuters wrap except for the DAX, where Reuters cites -0.3% at the 17:30 CET Xetra cash close versus the Trading Economics CFD -0.98%

text: European equities closed broadly lower on Tuesday, June 23, 2026 in a tech-led selloff. The pan-European STOXX Europe 600 ended 634.64, down 0.72% (Reuters -0.7%; MarketScreener 634.63 / -0.73%). The EURO STOXX 50 fell 1.25% to 6,232.46 from 6,311.32. France's CAC 40 dropped 0.71% to 8,340.71, a third straight decline. The UK's FTSE 100 was the relative outperformer, off just 9.00 points (-0.09%) to a precise 10,428.85. Germany's DAX carries a disclosed vendor discrepancy: the Trading Economics CFD-tracked DE40 closed 24,893.58, down 246.11 points (-0.98%) from 25,139.69, while the Reuters European cash-close wrap put the DAX at -0.3%; the gap reflects basis (Reuters 17:30 CET Xetra cash close versus the Trading Economics CFD extending into the post-cash session as the US tech selloff deepened). Daily moves are measured against the June 22 close.

market_session: London regular session close, Tuesday 2026-06-23 (16:35 BST)

calculation.method: close 10,428.85 minus prior close 10,437.85 equals -9.00 points; -9.00 / 10,437.85 equals -0.09 percent

text: The FTSE 100 is the cycle's one precise official close and the day's relative outperformer: on the Investors Chronicle/LSEG historical daily table it opened 10,438.24, traded a 10,332.40 to 10,462.19 range, and closed 10,428.85 on volume of 886,362,424 shares, down just 9.00 points (-0.09%) from Monday's 10,437.85 - well ahead of the continental indexes, whose deeper losses tracked the technology selloff (Reuters put the FTSE 100 at -0.2%).

subject.name: Global tech selloff and rising Fed rate-hike bets drive European equities lower

value.primary_support: global technology-sector selloff on AI-capex and valuation concerns, plus rising expectations of imminent US Federal Reserve rate hikes
triggers: SpaceX raising more cash after its IPO
SK Hynix signalling slower AI-chip output to favour commodity DRAM
value.rates: markets priced roughly 50 bps of Fed hikes by year-end and an additional 25 bps from the ECB
value.oil_iran: US Treasury issued a 60-day license authorizing sale of Iranian oil, pushing crude to a three-month low
value.macro: euro-area June flash PMI showed private-sector activity contracting for a third straight month; German activity shrank the most since 2024

text: European shares fell on Tuesday in a broad risk-off move led by a global technology selloff and rising bets on imminent US Federal Reserve rate hikes. Per Reuters, expectations for Fed hikes and concerns over corporate AI spending dented sentiment, with the STOXX 600 tech sector down 3.7%, its biggest daily drop since February. Trading Economics tied the chip-led leg to SpaceX raising more cash after its IPO and SK Hynix signalling it would slow AI-chip production to favour commodity DRAM, with markets pricing about 50 bps of Fed hikes by year-end and a further 25 bps from the ECB. A US Treasury 60-day license authorizing sale of Iranian oil pushed crude to a three-month low, and euro-area June flash PMI showed private-sector activity contracting for a third straight month - the steepest contraction in Germany since 2024.

laggards.technology_note: biggest weight on the STOXX 600; biggest daily drop since February
laggards.industrials_datacenter_exposed: Schneider Electric -4.53%, Siemens Energy -3.93%, Hochtief -4.3%, Siemens -2.42%

leaders.pharma_note: Sanofi up about 1.5% after EU approval of its Cenrifki treatment

text: The session was a clean defensives-over-cyclicals rotation. Technology was the biggest weight on the STOXX 600, down 3.7% in its biggest daily drop since February, and mining fell 3.3% with lower precious-metal prices, per Reuters. Industrials with datacenter exposure were hit hard on Trading Economics data - Schneider Electric -4.53%, Siemens Energy -3.93%, Hochtief -4.3% and Siemens -2.42%. Defensives outperformed: healthcare rose 1.9% and food and beverage 1.7%, with Sanofi up about 1.5% after the EU approved its Cenrifki treatment. Sector and single-stock percentages are carried at medium confidence; the index-level closes are high confidence.

Chipmakers led the decliners: STMicroelectronics fell about 8.1% to 8.5% (Trading Economics France -8.1%; Reuters -8.5%), Infineon -6.26% to 80.56, and ASML -5.74%. Datacenter- and capex-exposed industrials followed - Schneider Electric -4.53%, Hochtief -4.3%, Siemens Energy -3.93% and Siemens -2.42% - while Vonovia fell 3.21% after announcing a convertible-bond issue. Gainers were led by Airbus (+1.97%, intraday wrap +2.2%), SAP (+1.74% on the table, +2.13% in the Trading Economics news item), Qiagen (+1.51%) and Sanofi (+1.35%); luxury was mixed, with LVMH up 0.49%. Single-stock percentages are carried at medium confidence.
